Ole Lehmann
@itsolelehmann
Ultimate guide to Claude Cowork for non-technical people (15 minute setup to save 20 hours/week)
I'm going to show you how to set up Claude so it creates your documents, builds your presentations, and handles your recurring busywork automatically, without touching a terminal or writing any code.
The whole setup takes about 15 minutes.
If you've been anywhere near AI Twitter the last few months, you've probably seen people hyping Claude Code, Claude Cowork, agentic workflows, MCP, all of it.
And if you're like most people I talk to, you're thinking: okay, but what do I actually use? Where do I start? What's real and what's noise?
I want to cut through that for you.
I set up Cowork the week it launched and within a month it became the first thing I open every morning. Before email, before Notion, before anything. It builds my presentations, processes my data, drafts my content, connects to my Slack and Drive, and handles recurring tasks while I'm not even looking at it.
And I am not a developer. I run Claude Code workshops, I know my way around it, but I also know that most of my audience doesn't want to touch a terminal. You shouldn't have to. Cowork is the version of Claude built for people who just want the work done.
Let me show you exactly how to set it up and what to do with it once it's running.

WHERE COWORK SITS

Before anything else, it helps to understand the 3 options because they do very different things, and picking the wrong one is why a lot of people feel like AI "doesn't really work" for them.
Claude Chat is what you're probably already using. The text box at
claude.ai
. You type a question or a task, Claude replies with text, and then you go do the actual work yourself. You take that text, copy it, paste it into a Google Doc, format it, save it. Claude did the thinking. You did everything else.
For quick questions, brainstorming, writing a first draft? Chat is great. But the moment you need Claude to actually produce something (a formatted document, a spreadsheet, a presentation), you hit a wall. Chat can't touch your files. It can't save anything to your computer. You're always the middleman.
Claude Code is the most powerful option. You can access it right inside the Claude desktop app (it's one of the tabs at the top, same as Cowork) or through a standalone terminal. It has full access to your computer and can write code, run scripts, manage projects, deploy things.
The tradeoff is a steeper learning curve.
If you're comfortable with technical workflows or willing to learn, it's incredibly capable. But it does take some technical comfort to get the most out of.
Cowork sits in the middle and I think it strikes the best balance for most people. You point it at a folder on your computer, tell it what you need, and it reads your files, creates new ones, edits existing ones, and saves everything directly to that folder.
The output is an actual file on your computer, ready to use.
It has roughly 80% of the capability of Code with almost none of the learning curve. This is why I think it's perfect for most non-technical knowledge workers. You can be productive with it from day one.

HOW TO SET IT UP (ABOUT 15 MINUTES)

Let's get it running on your computer first. That way everything I cover after this is something you can try as you read.
Step 1: Download the app. Go to
claude.com/download
and grab the desktop app (available for Mac and Windows).
You need a paid plan to use Cowork. Pro is $20/month and gives you access to everything I'm covering here. Max ($100-$200/month) gives you more usage if you end up using it heavily.
Step 2: Open Cowork. Once the app is installed, open it. At the top of the window you'll see tabs. Look for the one that says Cowork. Click it. This is where everything happens.
Step 3: Select a folder. You'll see a folder icon near the top of the Cowork interface (it looks like a small folder with a plus sign). Click it and choose a folder from your computer.
This is the folder Claude will read from and save files to. Pick a folder with real work in it, a project folder, a client folder, whatever you're currently working on. Everything Claude creates gets saved here.
Step 4: Set up your global instructions. This is the step most people skip, and it's the one that makes the biggest difference. Go to Settings (gear icon, bottom left of the app), find the Cowork section, and click Edit next to Global Instructions.
Write in:
  • Who you are and what you do ("I'm a freelance brand strategist working with DTC founders").
  • How you like Claude to communicate ("Be direct, no filler, ask me questions before starting any task").
  • Your output preferences ("Save documents as .docx, keep things concise unless I ask for detail").
This takes five minutes. It means Claude starts every session already knowing your preferences instead of treating you like a stranger.
Step 5: Give it your first real task. Don't test it with something fake. Give it something you actually need done. Try something like:
"Read all the files in this folder. Then help me [your actual task]. Before you start, ask me clarifying questions so we're aligned on the approach."
Watch what happens. Instead of guessing what you want (like every other AI tool), Cowork will pop up a set of specific questions to make sure it gets it right. Answer them, and it goes to work.
That's it. You're set up. Everything below is what you can do now that it's running.

WHAT YOU CAN ACTUALLY DO WITH IT

Every example below is something that regular Claude Chat can't do. These all require Cowork because they involve reading your actual files, working across multiple apps, or running tasks without you sitting there watching.
I'm listing these because when I first heard "Cowork does your work," I thought: okay, but what does that mean? So here's what it means, specifically.
Work across apps in a single session. This is probably the thing that surprised me most. Say you have quarterly data in an Excel file and you need it turned into a client presentation.
Without Cowork, that workflow looks like: export the data, paste it into Claude chat, ask for analysis, copy the output, open PowerPoint, manually build slides, fight with formatting for 45 minutes. It's tedious and it's slow.
In Cowork, you point it at the folder that has your Excel file, tell it you need a quarterly presentation, and it does the whole chain.
Pulls the data from Excel, runs the analysis, builds charts, and stitches everything into a PowerPoint deck. One session. No switching between apps or copy-pasting.
The PowerPoint integration is fully end-to-end now, which is worth calling out. Cowork reads existing decks, understands slide layouts, and creates new ones that actually look like a human made them.
Queue up multiple tasks and walk away. With regular chat, you give Claude one task, wait for the response, give it the next task, wait again. You're babysitting the whole time. Cowork lets you give it 3 tasks at once, go make coffee, and come back to all 3 done.
This sounds like a small convenience. It's probably the biggest time-saver once you start using it. Think about how much of your day is spent sitting in AI conversations just... waiting. Queuing strips all of that dead time out.
Schedule recurring tasks that run automatically. This dropped recently and it might be my favorite addition. You type /schedule in any Cowork session, tell Claude what to do and when, and it just runs on that schedule. Without you opening the app. Without you doing anything.
Morning brief that summarizes your inbox every day at 8am. Weekly report that pulls data from your project folder every Friday. Daily cleanup of your downloads.
Repurpose content across platforms. Hand Cowork a long-form video file and it resizes, crops, and compresses for different platforms using ffmpeg under the hood. It drafts the social copy to go with it. The video processing works well, though I'd still spot-check output dimensions before posting.

SKILLS AND PLUGINS

Okay, this section matters more than the use cases above. Everything I just described, Cowork can do out of the box. But out of the box, Cowork is a generalist.
It's smart, but it doesn't know your work. It doesn't know your writing voice, your report format, your industry's terminology, or how your team expects deliverables to look.
Skills and plugins are how you fix that. And I think most people are skipping right past them, which is why so many early reactions to Cowork land at "it's fine, I guess" instead of "I can't go back to how I was working before."
Skills are simple text files (with a .md extension, which is just a plain text format that Claude reads really well) that teach Claude specific playbooks. A few examples of what people are building:
  • A brand voice skill that knows your tone, your phrases, what sounds wrong to you, so every piece of writing Claude produces already sounds like you wrote it.
  • A report formatting skill that matches your company's template: correct headers, correct fonts, data presented the way your team expects.
  • A cold outreach skill that knows your ICP, your value props, your sign-off, so when you ask Cowork to draft a sequence it doesn't start from scratch every time.
  • An analysis skill that knows which metrics you care about, how you like charts formatted, and what "good" looks like for your specific business.
Cowork scans what you're doing and pulls in relevant skills automatically. So if you've built that cold outreach skill and you ask Cowork to draft emails, it already knows everything.
You don't re-explain yourself every session. The skill loads silently and shapes everything Claude produces.
How to add skills: click the Customize button in the Cowork sidebar, then go to Skills.
You can browse community skills and install them with a click, or create your own.
To build a custom skill, create a markdown file (.md) that describes how you want Claude to handle a specific type of task: your rules, your preferences, your examples of good output
(I usually use voice dictation to ramble and describe what I want the skill to do, then have Claude ask me follow-up questions until it has enough context, then I have it generate the .md file for me).
Add it through the Skills section in Customize, and Cowork will pull it in automatically whenever it's relevant.
Plugins go a step further. They bundle skills, connectors, and tools into installable packages designed for specific roles. Anthropic shipped 11 official plugins at launch: Productivity, Marketing, Sales, Finance, Data Analysis, Legal, Product Management, Customer Support, Enterprise Search, Biology Research, and more coming.
What this means in practice:
  • Install the Sales plugin and Claude can research accounts, prep for calls, and draft outreach sequences using your ICP criteria.
  • Install the Data plugin and it explores your dataset, writes SQL, and flags anomalies.
  • Install the Finance plugin and it reads balance sheets and calculates ratios like it went to business school.
You don't need to be technical. You click install and Claude gets specialized knowledge for that domain.
Here's how to actually find and set these up (because the UI isn't obvious and it tripped me up at first):
For plugins: click the + button in the chat bar at the bottom of any Cowork session, then hit Plugins to browse what's available.
You can also go to
claude.com/plugins
for the full directory. Pick one that fits your role, click install. Once it's installed, type / in any session to see the new slash commands that plugin added. That's it.
For connectors (these are the integrations with Slack, Google Drive, Notion, and 50+ other tools): this is the part that took me an embarrassingly long time to find. Connectors don't live in Settings where you'd expect.
They're under the Customize button in the Cowork sidebar menu.
Click Customize, browse the connector directory, pick the tool you want, authenticate once.
After that, Claude can pull live data from that tool in every session. No more screenshotting your Slack threads. No more downloading docs from Drive just to re-upload them into Claude.
Connectors are free on all paid plans. Kind of wild that more people aren't using them.
You can also build your own skills and customize existing plugins. This is where it gets sort of addictive (I've spent more time than I'd like to admit tweaking my writing skill to get the voice exactly right).
The bottom line on skills and plugins: once they're dialed in, Cowork knows your preferences, your formats, your workflows. That's the gap between people who think Cowork is "meh" and people who can't stop talking about it.

WHERE IT FALLS SHORT

I want to be upfront about the tradeoffs because they're real and every hype post about Cowork seems to skip them.
Every session starts completely fresh. This is the single biggest friction point. When you close a Cowork session and open a new one, Claude has zero memory of who you are, what you were working on, or anything you discussed. Nothing carries over.
The workaround is solid: keep your context in markdown files inside your working folder, set up global instructions (Settings > Cowork > Edit next to Global Instructions) so Claude loads your identity and preferences at the start of each session. But it's a workaround. If you're used to tools that remember your last conversation, this will feel jarring at first.
Token efficiency is worse than Chat or Code. Cowork uses more of your tokens (basically your usage allowance) than regular chat.
There's a lot happening behind the scenes you don't see: it takes screenshots of its own work to check quality, processes images, manages its environment. All of that costs credits.
If you're on Pro and using it heavily every day, you'll notice. Save Cowork for the complex multi-step stuff and use regular chat for quick questions. If you're hitting limits constantly, Max ($100-$200/month) gives you more room. I highly recommend Max (most "worth it" subscription of all time IMO).
You get less visibility into what it's doing. Claude Code shows you every step in the terminal, so you can redirect the second something goes sideways. Cowork operates with more autonomy. That's great when it's working correctly, but it means your instructions need to be precise upfront.
Be explicit about what you want. "Clean up this folder" is how you end up with deleted files (this happened to someone, it went viral, and the lesson is real). "Organize files by date into subfolders, don't delete anything, and show me what you changed before executing" is a prompt that actually protects your stuff.
Skill chaining is more limited than Code. Claude Code users can build complex workflows where one skill's output feeds directly into the next skill automatically. Cowork's skill system is powerful but more linear right now. If you need deeply chained automation, Code is still the sharper tool for that.
For code, deployment, git, or system-level work, Code wins. Cowork runs sandboxed (basically in a protected environment) for safety, which is a smart design choice. But it means developers shouldn't expect it to replace their Code setup. Different tools for different jobs.
The honest summary: Claude Code gives you more power, more visibility, better token efficiency, and more control. Cowork gives you most of the practical value with almost none of the learning curve.
For knowledge workers who aren't writing code all day, that tradeoff is worth it.
